data.title: Obamacare premiums are said to rise 30% on average next year
data.description:
Managed care organizations operating in the Affordable Care Act's health insurance marketplace, Healthcare.gov, are set to increase premiums for their most popular coverage plans by 30% next year, The Washington Post reported on Friday.

Based on rates approved by the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services and shown in documents, the publication noted that the increase in prices will be the largest annual premium increase in recent years.

The higher premiums, coupled with expiring pandemic-era ACA subsidies, are expected to cause a twofold or even a threefold increase in health insurance payments for up to 17M Americans who purchase coverage plans through the federal marketplace.

The analysis based on average premiums for certain mid-level "silver" plans comes as the Healthcare.gov website is set to open next month, allowing Americans to compare different ACA plans during the open enrollment period, which starts on Nov. 1.

Since the launch of ACA marketplaces in 2014, Healthcare.gov premiums have risen only in 2018, when average benchmark plans climbed 34% from the prior year, according to the healthcare policy research organization KFF.

However, compared to 2018, ACA enrollees are set to face a more significant rise in out-of-pocket expenses next year, given the potential expiration of enhanced subsidies at the end of the year, a sticking point in the ongoing government shutdown.

Elevance Health (NYSE:ELV), UnitedHealth Group (NYSE:UNH), CVS Health's Aetna (CVS), Centene (CNC), Molina Healthcare (NYSE:MOH), and Oscar Health (NYSE:OSCR) are among the largest insurers with ACA marketplace businesses.

data.title: Southside Bancshares signals improved net interest income outlook following $325M securities restructuring
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Southside Bancshares, Inc. (SBSI) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Lee Gibson highlighted the sale of approximately $325 million of lower-yielding long-duration municipal and mortgage-backed securities, noting, “The net proceeds from these sales partially funded loan growth during the quarter with the balance reinvested in agency mortgage-backed pools...with primarily 5.5% and 6% coupons and, to a lesser extent, Texas municipal securities with coupons ranging from 5% to 5.75%.” Gibson stated, “The sale of these securities will not only enhance future net interest income, but it also provides for additional balance sheet flexibility as we grow. We estimate the payback of this loss to be less than 4 years.”
  • Gibson added, “Linked quarter, our net interest income increased $1.45 million, and our net interest margin decreased 1 basis point due to the issuance of the subordinated debt during the quarter.”
  • President Keith Donahoe reported, “Third quarter new loan production totaled approximately $500 million compared to the second quarter production of $290 million. Of the new loan production, $281 million approximately funded during the third quarter, including the $81 million Lee referenced, which closed on the last day of the quarter.”
  • Donahoe indicated, “Credit quality remains strong. During the third quarter, nonperforming assets increased approximately $2.7 million but remain concentrated in the previously disclosed $27.5 million multifamily loan that was moved into the nonperforming category during the first quarter.”
  • CFO Julie Shamburger reported, “For the third quarter, we reported net income of $4.9 million, a decrease of $16.9 million or 77.5%. Diluted earnings per share were $0.16 for the third quarter, a decrease of $0.56 per share linked quarter.”
  • Shamburger also noted, “Deposits increased $329.6 million or 5% on a linked quarter basis due to an increase in broker deposits of $288.6 million and a $137.1 million increase in commercial and retail deposits, partially offset by a decrease in public fund deposits of $96.1 million.”

Outlook

  • Gibson stated, “The NIM in the fourth quarter, I expect to be up slightly...I expect net interest income to improve nicely. And I think we're set up for a lot of positive things in the future when it comes to net interest income and the NIM.”
  • Management expects the unfunded portion of the quarter’s loan production to fund over the next 6 to 9 quarters, likely weighted towards the back end due to the construction nature.
  • Shamburger said, “At this time, we expect noninterest expense to be in the $38 million range for the fourth quarter.”

Financial Results

  • Shamburger reported, “As of September 30, loans were $4.77 billion, a linked quarter increase of $163.4 million or 3.5%.”
  • The average rate of loans funded during the third quarter was approximately 6.7%.
  • Allowance for credit losses increased to $48.5 million for the linked quarter from $48.3 million at June 30.
  • Shamburger stated, “Our securities portfolio was $2.56 billion at September 30, a decrease of $174.2 million or 6.4% from $2.73 billion last quarter due to the partial restructuring of the AFS portfolio.”
  • The net unrealized loss in the AFS securities portfolio was $15.4 million, a decrease of $45 million compared to $60.4 million last quarter.
  • Tax equivalent net interest margin was 2.94%, a decrease of 1 basis point on a linked-quarter basis.
  • Shamburger noted, “We repurchased 26,692 shares of our common stock at an average price of $30.24 during the third quarter.”

Q&A

  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: Asked about expected normalized margin for Q4 given late quarter loan growth and restructuring. Gibson responded, “The NIM in the fourth quarter, I expect to be up slightly...I expect net interest income to improve nicely. And I think we're set up for a lot of positive things in the future when it comes to net interest income and the NIM.”
  • Rose, Raymond James: Inquired about M&A and hiring opportunities. Gibson replied, “What we're looking at really hasn't changed. There are a few institutions that we have some interest in that potentially might be for sale. And in terms of hires, that is something we're looking at, and we've made a few hires...we hope to jump on that opportunity and make some additional hires there.”
  • Wood Lay, KBW: Asked about loan growth pipeline. Gibson said, “The pipeline is strong...after we took a dip in the pipeline that it bounced back up to $1.8 billion, which I feel is a really strong number.”
  • Lay, KBW: Inquired about pricing competition. Shamburger explained, “There's a lot of competition out there, both from the CRE standpoint and C&I. So we're not immune to it. We are being disciplined in our pricing approach.”
  • Lay, KBW: Questioned if further securities restructuring is planned. Gibson replied, “If the market allows and conditions are such that it makes sense to do some additional restructuring in the available-for-sale portfolio, we're certainly going to take a look at it...I would say most of the heavy lifting in the AFS portfolio has been done.”
  • Jordan Ghent, Stephens: Asked about future buybacks. Shamburger said, “When we see the price dip and it's opportunistic, we will be out there actively purchasing shares.”
  • Ghent, Stephens: Queried about trust fee income. Gibson commented, “We anticipate seeing double-digit revenue growth in that area next year as well.”
  • Ghent, Stephens: Asked about rate cut assumptions. Gibson said, “Next year, I'm anticipating probably at least 2 cuts...it could be more than 2 cuts next year.”
  • Anja Pelshaw, Hovde Group: Asked about DDA growth. Shamburger responded, “We do think that's going to moderate probably in the fourth quarter.”
  • Pelshaw, Hovde Group: Asked about C&I growth from new hires. Shamburger detailed, “We haven't been able to see truly a full year of production yet, but it's been positive. They are gathering deposits as well as loan growth right now.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a generally inquisitive but neutral tone, focusing on margin outlook, loan growth, competition, and M&A opportunities. There were no notably negative or skeptical lines of questioning.
  • Management’s tone was confident and forward-looking in both prepared remarks and Q&A, with Gibson stating, “we're set up for a lot of positive things in the future when it comes to net interest income and the NIM.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment remained confident from management, though the discussion of a large one-time loss created a slightly more measured tone. Analyst sentiment remained neutral across both quarters.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • This quarter saw the repositioning of $325 million in securities and a one-time net loss of $24.4 million, which was not present in the previous quarter.
  • Net income and EPS both declined sharply compared to Q2, primarily due to the securities sale loss, while net interest income increased.
  • Loan growth accelerated with $163 million linked quarter increase, up from $35 million prior quarter.
  • The loan pipeline, while rebounding to $1.8 billion, is below last quarter’s $2.1 billion but remains elevated versus the prior year.
  • Noninterest expense decreased from $39.3 million to $37.5 million.
  • Management’s outlook remains optimistic, especially regarding net interest income and NIM, despite the large securities restructuring.
  • Strategic focus has shifted toward balance sheet flexibility and selective hiring, with continued attention to M&A opportunities.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing competitive pressures in both CRE and C&I lending, with Shamburger noting, “There's a lot of competition out there, both from the CRE standpoint and C&I.”
  • The impact of rising rates on subordinated debt costs and the expected repricing of $92.1 million in subordinated notes was highlighted as a potential headwind.
  • The previously disclosed $27.5 million multifamily loan remains the primary nonperforming asset, though management continues to anticipate a resolution before year-end.
  • Shamburger indicated that some recent DDA growth may moderate in the fourth quarter.

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that the third quarter was defined by the proactive restructuring of the securities portfolio, which is expected to improve future net interest income and provide greater balance sheet flexibility. Despite a significant one-time loss impacting near-term profitability, the company highlighted robust loan growth, a strong pipeline, disciplined expense management, and continued focus on strategic hiring and M&A opportunities. The outlook for the coming quarters is optimistic, particularly for net interest income and margin expansion, supported by a healthy Texas market environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Eastern Bankshares plans new 5% share repurchase amid merger integration and commercial lending growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Eastern Bankshares, Inc. (EBC) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Robert Rivers, Executive Chairman, highlighted the milestone of Eastern’s fifth anniversary as a public company, noting that "today, Eastern is a $25.5 billion organization with the fourth largest deposit market share in Greater Boston, and we are the largest independent bank headquartered in Massachusetts." He emphasized intentional market expansion, community engagement, and consistent investment in talent as key drivers of improved earnings and shareholder returns.
  • CEO Denis Sheahan announced, "We are very pleased to have received the required regulatory approvals for our merger with HarborOne which is on track for a November 1 close." He described this as strengthening Eastern’s presence in Greater Boston and expanding into Rhode Island. Sheahan also announced the resumption of the share buyback program, stating, "Third quarter operating earnings of $74.1 million increased 44% from a year ago and generated solid returns." He cited a 1.16% operating return on assets and an 11.7% operating return on average tangible common equity, both up year-over-year. He reported commercial loan growth and an approximate 10% increase in relationship managers over the past year.
  • Sheahan also noted, "Assets under management reached a record high of $9.2 billion in the third quarter driven by market appreciation and modest positive net flows." The Board authorized a new 5% share repurchase program of up to 11.9 million shares.
  • CFO R. Rosato stated, "We reported net income of $106.1 million or $0.53 per diluted share for the third quarter." He added, "Operating return on assets of 116 basis points and return on average tangible common equity of 11.7% were down from second quarter metrics, both meaningfully improved from a year ago."

Outlook

  • The merger with HarborOne is expected to close November 1, and management reiterated that "the original margin expansion and numbers that we put out back in April for the combined institution are still good numbers." CFO Rosato commented on deposit costs, stating, "as the Fed eases, we will work deposit costs down and target deposit betas like our experience during the most recent tightening cycle or about 45% to 50%, with lags relative to Fed actions."
  • Management confirmed the resumption of the share buyback program and emphasized focus on organic growth and return of capital to shareholders. There were no significant changes in strategic direction compared to prior guidance.

Financial Results

  • CFO Rosato reported, "Net income of $106.1 million or $0.53 per diluted share for the third quarter." Operating earnings were $74.1 million or $0.37 per diluted share. Net interest income was $200.2 million, with margin at 3.47%. Excluding net discount accretion, net interest income would have increased approximately 3% quarter-over-quarter.
  • Noninterest income of $41.3 million declined from the prior quarter, primarily due to lower retirement benefit investment income and miscellaneous income. Wealth management fees contributed nearly half of noninterest income, with assets under management reaching $9.2 billion.
  • Noninterest expense rose to $140.4 million, with $3.2 million in merger-related costs. Deposits ended at $21.1 billion, down slightly from Q2, while loans grew 1.3% linked quarter, driven by commercial lending.
  • Asset quality remained strong, with net charge-offs to average loans at 13 basis points and allowance for loan losses at $233 million. One office loan accounted for a rise in nonperforming loans, but management stated, "we continue to believe the worst of the office loan problems is mostly behind us."

Q&A

  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: Asked about margin outlook and deposit cost pressures. CFO Rosato responded, "the competition has heated up here... I think that pressure remains in Q4. So that leads me to roughly flat deposit costs with a little bit of a wildcard on the asset side."
  • Del Monte questioned expense trends. Rosato replied, "I think we were a little inflated on the comp line this quarter. I think that will tend to settle down in Q4."
  • Del Monte inquired about M&A appetite. CEO Sheahan responded, "We have no plans in terms of additional mergers in the near term. But... if a merger opportunity were to arise, it's in our shareholders' best interest for us to evaluate the opportunity."
  • Mark Fitzgibbon, Piper Sandler: Asked for a breakdown of wealth management AUM growth. Rosato answered, "it was predominantly market-driven... net flows in the quarter were a little over $50 million positive."
  • Fitzgibbon asked about RIA acquisitions. Sheahan replied, "no, we're not interested at this point in any kind of M&A there."
  • Fitzgibbon mentioned a Holdco presentation. Sheahan stated, "we welcome that dialogue from whomever. But... we're excited about the future of the company."
  • Laura Havener Hunsicker, Seaport Research: Asked about office loan nonperformers. Sheahan explained, "It's one loan... we started building reserves that will mature next year."
  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen: Asked about NIM sustainability. Rosato noted, "competition in our market remains intense... Our message is, in the near term, a little slow, a little slower to maintain and eventually grow market share, but longer term... we should expect us to achieve our full betas."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on margin sustainability, expense management, and strategic direction, with a neutral to slightly cautious tone as they sought clarity on deposit costs, M&A priorities, and credit quality.
  • Management maintained a generally confident tone in prepared remarks, highlighting strategic progress and capital strength. In Q&A, management was measured and occasionally defensive, emphasizing focus on organic growth and prudent capital allocation, as seen in responses like "we have no plans in terms of additional mergers in the near term."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a consistent tone, with analysts continuing to probe for potential risks and management reiterating confidence in the company’s strategy and risk controls.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language was consistent with the previous quarter, reiterating a focus on organic growth and margin management. The share buyback was resumed, and no new M&A initiatives were announced, marking continuity in strategic direction.
  • Loan growth continued, with commercial lending remaining the key driver. Wealth management AUM increased further to a new record. Deposit costs rose modestly, and margin declined slightly, primarily due to lower discount accretion.
  • Analysts maintained focus on margin, expense trends, and credit quality, similar to the prior quarter. Management’s confidence in the commercial lending and wealth management strategy remained strong.
  • There were no significant shifts in management or analysts’ sentiment compared to the previous quarter. Management reiterated that the worst of the office loan issues is likely over, similar to prior reassurances.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited heightened competition for deposits in the region as a key challenge, with CFO Rosato stating, "the competition has heated up here."
  • Slightly elevated noninterest expense, driven by higher compensation and merger costs, was acknowledged. Management expects compensation expenses to moderate in the next quarter.
  • Office loan exposure remains under scrutiny, but management reported that the primary increase in nonperforming loans was due to a single, long-monitored loan.
  • Integration of the HarborOne merger was mentioned as a significant ongoing initiative, with management reiterating focus on a smooth transition and delivery of anticipated synergies.

Final Takeaway

Eastern Bankshares management emphasized the successful regulatory approval for the HarborOne merger, continued commercial lending momentum, and a record in wealth management assets as critical drivers of future growth. The resumption of the share buyback and strong capital metrics signal management’s confidence in continued shareholder value creation, while discipline in expense control and deposit pricing remains a priority as competitive pressures persist. The company’s ongoing strategic investments and organic growth orientation are positioned to support sustained profitability and market leadership in the quarters ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Customers Bancorp outlines 13%–15% net interest income growth for 2025 while expanding noninterest-bearing deposits to 31%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Customers Bancorp (CUBI) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Jay Sidhu, Founder, Chairman & CEO, highlighted, "We experienced deposit-led growth in our balance sheet of more than $1.5 billion over the quarter, delivered positive operating leverage and strengthened our already robust capital levels through a very successful common equity offering that was oversubscribed by about 10x." He emphasized the company's industry-leading EPS and book value compounding and cited Customers Bancorp as the "#1 performing bank stock in the United States for institutions over $10 billion in assets over a 5-year period."
  • President Samvir Sidhu reported, "We generated $1.4 billion of deposit growth, led by our new commercial banking teams and cubiX clients. Our loan growth was 6% quarter-over-quarter with diversified contributions across multiple verticals." He added, "Our net interest margin expanded meaningfully by 19 basis points quarter-over-quarter, and our net interest income increased by 14% in the quarter."
  • Samvir Sidhu noted, "We compounded tangible book value at a 25% annualized pace in the quarter to nearly $60 per share, continuing our multiyear trend of 15% annualized growth."
  • CFO Mark McCollom stated, "Net interest margin expanded by 19 basis points this quarter to 3.46%, marking the fourth consecutive quarter of improvement. Our net interest income increased by about 14% to $202 million for the quarter."

Outlook

  • Mark McCollom announced, "For deposits, we are increasing the full year growth range to 8% to 10% for the year, up from 5% to 9% given the momentum we experienced during the quarter. For loans, we are increasing full year growth to 13% to 14%, up from 8% to 11% previously."
  • The company raised its net interest income growth target: "We are now projecting our net interest income to grow between 13% and 15% for the year, up from 7% to 10% previously."
  • McCollom also projected, "Our efficiency ratio will be below 50% for the year versus 56% in 2024."
  • Common equity Tier 1 ratio is expected to be around 13% at the end of 2025.

Financial Results

  • Customers Bancorp delivered core EPS of $2.20, with a core ROE and ROA of 15.5% and 1.25%, respectively.
  • Total revenues reached $232 million, up 12% for the quarter.
  • The company reported a 22% increase in third quarter EPS, and a 64% rise from the same period a year ago.
  • Noninterest expenses declined to $105.2 million, with the efficiency ratio improving to 45.4%.
  • Tangible book value per share grew to $59.72, up 6.2% sequentially.
  • The common equity Tier 1 ratio improved by 100 basis points to 13%, while tangible common equity grew 50 basis points to 8.4%.
  • Nonperforming assets were 25 basis points of total assets; allowance for credit loss sits at 103 basis points, representing 534% coverage of nonperforming loans.

Q&A

  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen, asked about the pace of deposit growth from new banking team hires in 2026. Samvir Sidhu responded, "We would expect that pace to continue in 2026 based upon the '23 and '24 teams. The 25 teams are really going to start adding balances in the sort of end of the first half to the middle of next year and really ramp up."
  • Lee inquired on cubiX deposit growth and its strategic value. Samvir Sidhu explained the platform's continued institutional activity and stated, "In October, our levels are about where they were in the third quarter. I'd also say that October...is on track to be our highest cubiX month ever in terms of network volume and activity."
  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James, questioned the potential increase in fees from the cubiX platform. Samvir Sidhu replied, "We will explore fees. Right now, deposit growth is far outpacing any type of 'asset sensitivity' of noninterest-bearing deposits that are held in cash."
  • Moss asked about margin pressure from rate cuts. Mark McCollom answered, "For a 25 basis point rate move, it's around $1.5 million annualized impact to our NII. But we think...there will be sufficient growth to make up any NIM compression."
  • Peter Winter, D.A. Davidson, asked about expenses. Mark McCollom explained, "On the professional fees, yes, we continue to build out and invest in our risk infrastructure and work through the agreement... On the FDIC expenses...as we continue to work through and derisk our balance sheet, we are making progress in ultimately getting reductions in our FDIC insurance."
  • David Bishop, Hovde Group, inquired about CRE concentration. Samvir Sidhu said, "We still remain below 200%." He also shared, "our deposits are greater than loans since the third quarter of last year when the team started originating."
  • Kelly Motta, KBW, focused on asset quality and NDFI lending. Mark McCollom explained, "NDFI loans generally fall into 3 categories: Mortgage warehouse and what we call fund finance or capital call lending...and lender finance...we've not only had 0 losses, but 0 loan defaults."
  • Matthew Breese, Stephens, asked about stablecoin exposure. Samvir Sidhu stated, "Our view doesn't change and hasn't changed quarter-to-quarter...it's adding interest income to our platform and continuing to strengthen the value of our overall franchise and sort of earnings base."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions regarding deposit growth sustainability, cubiX platform expansion, fee income opportunities, expense management, asset quality, and CRE concentration, maintaining a neutral to slightly positive tone and seeking clarification on growth drivers and risk management.
  • Management's tone was confident in both prepared remarks and responses, frequently referencing outperformance and competitive differentiation. Phrases like "we are incredibly excited about the prospects" and "we have the strongest payments network" reflected a high level of confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management maintained its confident tone, while analysts' questions continued to probe for details on growth sustainability, risk, and strategic direction without significant skepticism.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for deposit growth, loan growth, and net interest income was raised from the previous quarter, signaling improved momentum.
  • Strategic focus remained on deposit transformation, cubiX platform growth, and team recruitment, with new verticals added in title and sports/entertainment.
  • Key financial metrics such as net interest margin, noninterest expense, and efficiency ratio all improved sequentially.
  • Management tone stayed confident, while analysts maintained focus on granularity of growth, risk concentrations, and monetization opportunities.
  • The company reported higher sequential growth rates in EPS, loan growth, and tangible book value.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited potential deposit concentration, asset sensitivity to rate moves, and regulatory changes as areas of ongoing focus.
  • Analysts highlighted concerns regarding the sustainability of deposit growth, the scalability and risk of cubiX, CRE concentration, and potential impacts from further rate cuts.
  • Mitigation strategies included maintaining cash holdings on cubiX deposits, strong credit discipline, diversified loan portfolios, and continued investment in risk and compliance infrastructure.

Final Takeaway

Customers Bancorp reported a quarter marked by robust deposit and loan growth, expansion of its cubiX payments platform, and improved capital and profitability metrics. The company revised its full-year guidance upward across key indicators, while maintaining strong credit performance and a disciplined approach to growth. Management expressed confidence in sustaining momentum into year-end and 2026, supported by a growing team, strategic investments, and the ongoing transformation of the bank’s operating model.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Soft CPI data powers Wall Street to a record high as S&P 500 hits 6,800 for the first time
data.description:
Wall Street on Friday soared after market participants, starved of economic data during the ongoing U.S. government shutdown, received a reprieve in the form of a cooler-than-expected consumer inflation report.

All three major averages scaled new intraday all-time peaks, with the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) surpassing the 6,800 points level for the first time ever. The gauge was last +1% in afternoon trade. Meanwhile, the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +1.1%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1.3%. 

Before the opening bell, data from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics showed headline CPI coming in at +0.3% M/M, versus an expected figure of +0.4%. Core CPI for September was +0.2% M/M, versus a consensus of +0.3%. On a Y/Y basis, core CPI was +3.0%, still well above the Federal Reserve's 2% target. 

The CPI print reinforced expectations that the Fed would deliver another 25 basis point interest rate cut at its monetary policy committee meeting next week. Meanwhile, the data could be the last inflation indicator for a while, as the White House said the October CPI report likely won't be released.

"Today’s CPI is consistent with a +0.2% MoM reading in the more important core PCE deflator (which will be delayed). That would make the third such tame reading in a row and the fifth in the past six months. The light is getting greener for the Fed to cut next week and keep on going until the 3% neutral Fed funds estimate, at the very least," David Rosenberg, founder and president of Rosenberg Research & Associates, said on X (formerly Twitter).

Diane Swonk, chief economist at KPMG US, cautioned that staffing shortages ahead of the government shutdown could be undermining the real-time value of the CPI data.

"The White House has warned that we may not get inflation data for October, given the shutdown. Some data is easier than other data to backfill. Inflation is not one of them," Swonk added.

Turning to the fixed-income markets, U.S. Treasury yields were largely lower across the board. They had initially slid as investors had snapped up bonds after the CPI report. The benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y) was last down 1 basis point to 4.00%, while the shorter-end, more rate-sensitive 2-year yield (US2Y) was also down 1 basis point to 3.49%.

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

Looking at the earnings season, shares of Procter & Gamble ((PG)) were +1.3% after the consumer brands giant topped quarterly organic sales estimates. 

Ford ((F)) stock was the top S&P 500 percentage gainer, up +11.7%. The legacy carmaker comfortably beat quarterly revenue expectations, though it warned of an up to $2B hit from a fire at a supplier plant.

data.title: Procter & Gamble outlines up to 4% organic sales growth for 2026 as restructuring targets agility and innovation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Procter & Gamble (PG) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Andre Schulten, Chief Financial Officer, opened with, "First quarter results reflect strong execution of our integrated strategy in a difficult geopolitical competitive and consumer environment. This marks 40 consecutive quarters of organic sales growth and keeps us on track for the tenth consecutive fiscal year of core EPS growth." He noted broad-based growth, with 8 of 10 product categories growing or holding organic sales, led by Skin & Personal Care, which was up high single digits. Volume was in line with the prior year, while pricing and mix were each up 1%.

  • Schulten highlighted, "We recently started shipments of Tide's biggest upgrade to liquid detergent in 20 years." The company is expanding Tide evo, developed on their Functional fibers platform, with online launches and incremental retail demand. Investments in innovation span multiple categories, including upgrades to U.S. Baby Care and premium body wash launches in Greater China.

  • The company is executing a restructuring program that includes reducing up to 7,000 nonmanufacturing roles, or up to 15% of the current nonmanufacturing workforce, over the fiscal year and fiscal '27. Schulten stated, "We're making very good progress with organization designs to deliver this objective."

  • Portfolio changes include shifting to an import model in Pakistan, discontinuing certain products in India, the Philippines, and enterprise markets, and focusing the Olay brand on the most productive European markets. Schulten explained these steps are designed to drive efficiency, faster innovation, and margin expansion.

Outlook

  • Schulten reported, "We're maintaining all guidance ranges for the fiscal year. Organic sales growth of in line to plus 4%." The guidance includes a 30 to 50 basis point headwind from product and market exits related to restructuring.

  • The company expects core EPS growth of in line to plus 4%, translating to a range of $6.83 to $7.09 per share, or $6.96 at the center. Modest earnings growth is anticipated over the balance of the year due to increased investment in innovation and competitiveness, especially in the U.S. and Europe.

  • The outlook factors in a commodity cost headwind of approximately $100 million after tax and a foreign exchange tailwind of $300 million after tax. Tariff costs are now expected to be $500 million before tax. Free cash flow productivity is forecasted at 85% to 90%, with $10 billion in dividends and $5 billion in stock repurchases planned, returning $15 billion to shareholders.

Financial Results

  • Core earnings per share for the quarter were $1.99, up 3% year-over-year. Core gross margin was down 50 basis points, and core operating margin was equal to the prior year. Productivity improvement was 230 basis points, and adjusted free cash flow productivity reached 102%.

  • $3.8 billion in cash was returned to shareowners, with $2.55 billion in dividends and $1.25 billion in share repurchases for the quarter.

Q&A

  • Dara Mohsenian, Morgan Stanley: Asked about the restructuring's internal reception and impact on competitiveness. Schulten responded, "We are right now perfectly on track on all elements of the restructuring execution... We'll end up with a faster-growing and more effective portfolio when we're done."

  • Peter Galbo, BofA Securities: Inquired about heightened competitiveness in North America Fabric Care and Baby Care. Schulten said both categories face intense promotion, but the company is "executing across both baby and Fabric Care" with integrated superiority, innovation, and retailer collaboration.

  • Lauren Lieberman, Barclays: Questioned market share declines. Schulten explained, "Global aggregate share... is down 30 basis points over the past 3 and past 6 months," but noted improving U.S. absolute share and confidence in exiting the year with share growth.

  • Stephen Powers, Deutsche Bank: Asked about China’s outlook. Schulten attributed progress to "fundamentally chang[ing] many of the variables that drive the business," including go-to-market and innovation.

  • Additional questions covered pricing strategies, regional outlooks, portfolio changes, and the impact of tariffs and competitive dynamics.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about competitive pressure, market share losses, and category deceleration, frequently probing for details on restructuring, pricing, and regional trends. The tone was neutral to slightly cautious, with pointed questions about how P&G would regain growth momentum.

  • Management maintained a confident and proactive tone during prepared remarks and the Q&A, emphasizing execution, innovation, and restructuring progress. Phrases such as "We are confident..." and "We believe..." were common, though Schulten acknowledged the challenges in competitive and consumer environments.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment remains cautious, with ongoing focus on share, competitiveness, and restructuring effects. Management’s tone is consistent, focusing on strategy and operational execution, with slightly more detail on restructuring progress in this quarter.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for organic sales and core EPS growth remains unchanged at in line to plus 4% from the prior quarter. The company reduced projected tariff headwinds from $1 billion to $500 million before tax.

  • Strategic focus has shifted further toward executing the restructuring program, with more concrete details on portfolio changes and workforce reductions.

  • Analysts continued to focus on competitiveness, innovation, and category trends, with persistent attention to market share and growth in North America and China.

  • Management’s confidence in the restructuring and innovation pipeline is more pronounced this quarter, emphasizing faster execution and agility.

Risks and Concerns

  • Challenges identified include heightened competitive activity in the U.S. and Europe, decelerating category consumption, and ongoing tariff and commodity cost volatility.

  • Management’s mitigation strategies involve accelerated innovation, targeted investments, portfolio restructuring, and supply chain enhancements.

  • Analysts expressed concerns about market share, competitive promotions, and the durability of improvements in China and Latin America.

Final Takeaway

Procter & Gamble’s fiscal 2026 outlook underscores a commitment to disciplined execution of its integrated strategy while navigating a challenging environment. The company is maintaining growth guidance, investing in innovation, and advancing a significant restructuring to enhance agility and efficiency. Management remains confident that these steps, combined with a robust innovation pipeline and targeted portfolio actions, will drive long-term growth and value creation for shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: USCB Financial signals $14.4M investment portfolio cash flow opportunity while targeting margin expansion into Q4
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: USCB Financial Holdings, Inc. (USCB) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Luis de la Aguilera, President, CEO & Chairman, highlighted record fully diluted EPS for the third consecutive quarter, noting, "For the quarter ended September 30, 2025, the bank posted net income of $8.9 million or $0.45 per diluted share, up from $6.9 million or $0.35 per share in the third quarter of 2024." He emphasized year-over-year growth in total assets and deposits, improved return on average assets and equity, and a strengthened efficiency ratio. De la Aguilera also pointed out the successful completion of a $40 million subordinated debt issuance, which funded the repurchase of approximately 2 million shares at $17.19 per share.
  • The CEO stated, "Liquidity remains strong and well above policy limits, providing ample flexibility to support loan growth and capital initiatives." He cited diversified deposit-focused business verticals accounting for $672 million, or 27% of total deposits, and affirmed, "Credit performance continues to be exceptionally strong. Nonperforming loans declined to just 0.06% of total loans, down from 0.14% last year."
  • Robert Anderson, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Net income was $8.9 million or $0.45 per diluted share, and that's up 29% over the prior year." Anderson explained the impact of the $40 million debt raise and share repurchase, noting that on a pro-forma basis, EPS would have been $0.49 if the buyback had occurred on day one of the quarter. He discussed the use of excess cash in the securities portfolio, interest rate collar hedging, and stable interest-bearing deposit costs.
  • William Turner, Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer, stated, "Allowance for credit losses is at $25 million at the third quarter end and at an adequate 1.17% of the portfolio... Nonperforming loans as of quarter end steady at $1.3 million and remained at 0.06% of the portfolio and are well covered by the allowance."

Outlook

  • Management expects net interest margin (NIM) improvement, with Anderson indicating, "the NIM for the month of September was 3.27%. All these items are good tailwinds heading into Q4."
  • The company anticipates that "rate cuts expected in the short term... will benefit our funding costs and overall margin and the effect of these rate cuts will be seen more predominantly in the fourth quarter" (Anderson).
  • Cash flows from the investment securities portfolio are expected to provide flexibility: "We expect to receive $14.4 million in cash flows from the portfolio for the remainder of 2025 at current rates and approximately $76.4 million in 2026 with a runoff rate of about 3%."

Financial Results

  • Net income for Q3 2025 was $8.9 million or $0.45 per diluted share. Return on average assets was 1.27%, and return on average equity was 15.74%. Net interest margin was 3.14%. Net interest income before provision for credit losses stood at $21.3 million.
  • Total assets reached $2.8 billion, total deposits were $2.5 billion, and loans held for investment grew to $2.1 billion.
  • Tangible book value per share increased to $11.55, up 6% from the prior year.
  • Total expenses were $13 million, including $188,000 in one-time legal and administration expenses. Efficiency ratio was 52.28%.
  • Noninterest income was 14.8% of total revenue and 0.52% to average assets.

Q&A

  • Wood Lay, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc.: "Just a question on the yacht payoffs you saw in the quarter... could you just quantify the amount of payoffs you saw in that division in the quarter, and when in the quarter they occurred?" Robert Anderson: "It was a little over $10 million, and that happened in August, and that impacted our loan yields in August and our margin in August."
  • Lay: "Is it a sign of sustained loan momentum entering the fourth quarter? Or was it September just a strong month?" Luis de la Aguilera: "Historically, we always see a seasonal dip in Q3... September was a record-setting month for the year. As we look forward, the go-forward pipeline is absolutely in line with what we've seen over the last 5 quarters."
  • Lay: "What are you seeing on the loan competition side, especially on pricing?" De la Aguilera: "We price to relationship... We are not a transactional lender. So every deal is priced based on opportunity and based on existing loan balances and deposit balances and overall relationship."
  • Feddie Strickland, Hovde Group, LLC: "Given that we have a little bit of additional cost... does the quarter still, I guess, end at that 3.27%..." Anderson: "3.27% or slightly better for the fourth quarter is still a realistic number."
  • Strickland: "What are you seeing on the SBA side, keeping in mind the government shutdown fees?" Anderson: "We probably had $200,000 that got slow walked at the end of the quarter that will fall into the first quarter."
  • Strickland: "What about the opportunity set on the condo association banking business line?" De la Aguilera: "We're very bullish about the association banking vertical. I think it's one of our greatest opportunities for scale... we believe that this is an area that we could probably double the book of business in the next 18 months."
  • Michael Rose, Raymond James & Associates, Inc.: "Is 3.27% kind of a good starting point to think about the fourth quarter?" Anderson: "We profile as liability sensitive. We have been aggressive on the rate cuts... I think we're well positioned for... any further cuts. So we would expect expansion on the NIM."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for quantification of special items and the sustainability of recent trends, displaying a neutral to slightly optimistic tone when discussing forward expectations for NIM and business vertical opportunities.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, frequently referencing consistency and record performance, with Anderson affirming, "3.27% or slightly better for the fourth quarter is still a realistic number," and de la Aguilera stating, "We are very bullish on this area" regarding association banking.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment shifted from cautious optimism about loan growth to more direct inquiries about margin sustainability and business-line growth. Management's tone was consistently confident both quarters, but more assertive about future expectations in Q3 2025.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Net income rose from $8.1 million in Q2 to $8.9 million in Q3. EPS climbed from $0.40 to $0.45. The efficiency ratio was stable, while the NIM dipped to 3.14% from 3.28% in Q2. Total deposits increased from $2.3 billion to $2.5 billion, and tangible book value per share edged up from $11.53 to $11.55. Credit quality metrics remained strong.
  • Strategic focus shifted in Q3 with the $40 million sub debt raise and significant share repurchase, while Q2 emphasized groundwork for growth with a universal shelf offering and new hires. Q3 placed more emphasis on margin management and investment portfolio flexibility.
  • Analysts in Q2 focused more on international deposit gathering and loan growth, while in Q3 they concentrated on margin trajectory, special items, and business-line scalability. Management's confidence in future earnings and margin expansion became more explicit in Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited a temporary mix shift in deposits, with DDA balances declining and higher reliance on interest-bearing deposits, putting some pressure on funding costs. Anderson anticipates improvement in the funding base in Q4 as liabilities reprice with rate cuts.
  • The payoff of yacht loans reduced loan yield for the quarter, though management expects new production to offset this in future quarters.
  • One-time expenses were noted, including legal fees and administration for the interest rate collar.
  • No significant loan losses or credit quality deterioration are expected.

Final Takeaway

USCB Financial Holdings' Q3 2025 performance demonstrated continued earnings growth, robust credit quality, and prudent capital management, marked by a major share repurchase and sustained investment in business verticals. Management remains optimistic about margin expansion into Q4 and 2026, underpinned by strong liquidity, scalable deposit platforms, and a flexible investment portfolio expected to generate $14.4 million in cash flow for the remainder of 2025. The company is focused on leveraging Florida's economic momentum while maintaining disciplined risk and expense management to support long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Social Security Administration lifts 2026 COLA benefit to 2.8%
data.description:
The Social Security cost-of-living adjustment ("COLA") will be raised to 2.8% next year from 2.5% in 2025, the Social Security Administration said on Friday.

COLA increases Social Security and Supplemental Security Income each year so that these payments maintain their purchasing power as prices rise. 

The 2.8% COLA will start with benefits payable to nearly 71M Social Security beneficiaries in January 2026. Increased payments to almost 7.5M SSI recipients will begin Dec. 31, 2025. Of course, some people receive payments from both programs.

In addition, the maximum amount of earnings subject to the Social Security tax (taxable maximum) will advance to $184,500 from $176,100, the SSA said in a press release.

data.title: Healthpeak signals $1B asset recycling window as lab pipeline doubles and tech investments drive cost savings
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Healthpeak Properties, Inc. (DOC) Q3 2025

Management View

  • President and CEO Scott Brinker described the past 60 days as “a turning point in our business,” noting, “Leading indicators in life science are turning positive and private market values for outpatient medical are strengthening. As a premier scaled owner in both businesses, we see significant value and upside when we look at our stock price today.”
  • Brinker highlighted the strategic $5 billion merger with Physicians Realty Trust, which established Healthpeak as a leading platform in the outpatient sector, enabled internal property management for 39 million square feet, and brought new growth opportunities: “We now own the tenant relationship and the local market knowledge. The internalization also allows us to deploy technology at the property level quickly and at scale.”
  • He announced that the company is negotiating transactions that “have the potential to generate proceeds of $1 billion or more,” with plans to recycle outpatient sale proceeds into higher-return lab opportunities as indicators turn positive in the sector.
  • Brinker introduced new executive Denis Sullivan to the life science business and investment strategy, citing deepened local leadership in core markets.
  • On the CCRC business, Brinker stated, “NOI is up more than 50%, including double-digit growth this year,” with sequential occupancy up 70 basis points and expectations for continued growth.
  • Chief Financial Officer Kelvin Moses said, “We’re advancing our strategic plan to strengthen our capabilities as an AI-enabled real estate owner with a leading investment management platform designed to meet our clients' needs across geographies and asset types.”
  • Moses reported, “We reported FFO as adjusted of $0.46 per share, AFFO of $0.42 per share and year-to-date portfolio same-store growth of 3.8%.”

Outlook

  • The company reaffirmed its FFO as adjusted and same-store expectations within the original guidance range.
  • Guidance for interest expense and G&A was reduced by $10 million, attributed to better pricing on senior notes issuances, technology-enabled productivity gains, and merger synergies.
  • Brinker reiterated, “Our leasing pipeline today is roughly 2x the pipeline at the start of the year.”

Financial Results

  • Financial and operating performance was in line with forecast, with FFO as adjusted at $0.46 per share and AFFO at $0.42 per share.
  • CCRC portfolio achieved cash NOI growth of 9.4% for the quarter, with year-over-year occupancy gains of 150 basis points.
  • In outpatient medical, 1.2 million square feet of leases were executed with 3% escalators or above and positive cash re-leasing spreads of 5.4%. Year-to-date, leasing volumes reached 3.2 million square feet and occupancy ended at 91%.
  • Lab segment executed 339,000 square feet of leases (45% new) with a positive 5% re-leasing spread; year-to-date leasing volumes totaled 1.1 million square feet and quarter-end occupancy was 81%.
  • The company issued $500 million in senior unsecured notes at 4.75%, ending the quarter at 5.3x net debt to adjusted EBITDA and $2.7 billion in liquidity.
  • Year-to-date, $158 million in asset sales and loan repayments were completed, with $204 million of dispositions under a purchase and sale agreement.

Q&A

  • Ronald Kamdem, Morgan Stanley: Asked about the lab leasing pipeline and mix of tenants. Brinker responded, “The quantum has doubled, but equally important the mix of new and renewal is much more favorable...Improved sentiment in the sector, improved capital raising...the trajectory, the momentum is very positive.”
  • Kamdem followed up on capital recycling. Brinker explained, “We have roughly $130 million undersigned contract at a really strong cap rate. We're working on a lot. We feel like it's an opportune time to take advantage of that buyer interest.”
  • Nicholas Yulico, Scotiabank: Inquired about lab portfolio leased vs. occupied rates. Moses stated, “Our total occupancy today in lab at 81% is largely in line with the occupied rate.”
  • Yulico also asked about the lab JV impairment. Moses clarified, “These are assets that we have high confidence in, we'll continue to own long term...the impairments, not cash, it doesn't impact FFO, but we thought it was prudent to do so this quarter.”
  • Farrell Granath, BofA Securities: Asked about the risk list evolution. Moses noted, “Our existing tenant base continues to access the capital markets...the exposure in our portfolio has come down, I would say, pretty meaningfully over the last 60 days.”
  • Granath also asked about AI demand for lab space. Brinker replied, “AI-native biotech research...has been very much a positive for our portfolio...the pipeline includes them as well.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns around lab occupancy, capital recycling, tenant risks, and the timing of sector recovery, showing a neutral to slightly positive tone as they acknowledged positive momentum but sought clarity on risks and execution.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing improved sentiment, “encouraging” trends, and “favorable signs.” Brinker used language such as “we see significant value and upside” and “we have the footprint, people and balance sheet to capture market share.”
  • Compared to the prior quarter, both analysts and management displayed increased optimism, with management expressing greater confidence in sector recovery and analysts focusing more on execution details than downside risk.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter emphasized an inflection point, with management citing a doubling of the leasing pipeline and a $1 billion asset recycling window, while the previous quarter focused more on defensive measures and monitoring capital markets for lab tenants.
  • Guidance language remained firm, with a reduction in expense guidance this quarter compared to reaffirmation in the prior quarter.
  • Analysts shifted from probing about tenant distress and capital raising challenges to questions about pipeline composition and recycling strategies.
  • Key metrics such as leasing volume and occupancy in both outpatient and lab businesses showed improvement or stabilization, while management’s confidence in technology-driven cost efficiencies and sector recovery increased noticeably.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged expected declines in occupancy over the next few months due to expirations and terminations, with Brinker stating, “our occupancy will decline for the next few months due to expirations and terminations.”
  • The impairment in the lab JV was discussed, with Moses noting it does not impact FFO.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the pace and impact of capital recycling, potential dilution, and the timing and magnitude of occupancy troughs before recovery.
  • Management outlined mitigation through a diversified tenant base, strong private market demand for assets, and disciplined capital allocation.

Final Takeaway

Healthpeak Properties signaled a strategic pivot fueled by a strong leasing pipeline, robust capital recycling initiatives targeting $1 billion or more in proceeds, and early signs of recovery in the life sciences sector. Management emphasized technology-driven efficiencies, disciplined capital allocation, and confidence in capturing market share as the sector rebounds. Despite near-term occupancy headwinds, the company’s leadership highlighted enhanced liquidity, expense reductions, and a unique window to redeploy capital into high-return opportunities as core drivers for future growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
